coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform based on ionic liquid carbon dots for rapid detection of amylase in urine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiujuan
Wang, Jiarui
Tian, Haojia
Wang, Chenzhao
Qiu, Xiaowen
Li, Zhicong
Wang, Zhibing
coredata.dc:description: Changes in amylase (AMY) concentration in urine serve a vital function in monitoring human health. However, the development of non-destructive, sensitive, and real-time assays for AMY concentration still faces serious challenges. In this study, a novel fluorescence/smartphone imaging colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform based on ionic liquid carbon dots (IL-CDs) was designed for the first time for assessing AMY concentration in urine. The obtained IL-CDs exhibited high sensitivity, good selectivity, and excellent stability. AMY can significantly decrease the fluorescence intensity through static quenching, enabling direct AMY concentration monitoring. Significantly, the proposed method was extended to the measurement of AMY in the urine samples with good spiked recoveries (96.98–103.81 %) as well as high accuracy. Furthermore, a smartphone-dependent detection method was developed after exploring the association between IL-CDs (G/(R + B)) fluorescence intensity at different concentrations of AMY, which simplified the AMY detection process. Through the combination of fluorescence and smartphone imaging colorimetry, it not only eliminated the damage to the human body during the detection process, but also realized the direct detection of AMY. The present method provided a fast and reliable new idea for monitoring human health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-mode
Amylase

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HR-TEM image of IL-CDs. (B) Particle size distribution IL-CDs. (C) XRD pattern of the IL-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of the IL-CDs.
(A)XPS spectrum of IL-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1 s, (C) N 1 s, (D) O 1 s, (E) F 1 s, and (F) B 1 s.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the IL-CDs. Inset: pictures of IL-CDs solution under white light (left) and UV lamp (right). (B) Fluorescence spectra with the excitation wavelength increasing from 400 to 460 nm.

(A) Effect of pH 1–14 on the fluorescence intensity. (B) Fluorescence stability of IL-CDs in different NaCl concentration. (C) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of IL-CDs at 365 nm UV irradiation. (D) Thermal stability on IL-CDs.

(A) Selectivity of CDs for AMY. (B) The fluorescence spectra of IL-CDs after adding different concentrations of AMY (0–0.01 g/mL). Inset: the linearity between fluorescence intensity at 440 nm and the AMY concentration (0–0.01 g/mL).

(A) UV–vis absorption (green line) of AMY, FL excitation (pink line) and FL emission (orange line) spectra of IL-CDs. (B) Fluorescence Lifetime of IL-CDs and IL-CDs + AMY. (C) The absorption spectrum of AMY and the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of the IL-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of IL-CDs, IL-CDs + AMY and AMY. (E) Stern-Volmer (SV) curves of IL-CDs on amylase at three different temperatures (303 K, 313 K, and 323 K). (F) Static quenching mechanism of IL-CDs.
Schematic description of IL-CDs for identification of AMY by smartphone imaging colorimetry.
Greenness of this method was assessed by (A) AGREE; (B) AGREEprep.
Schematic representation of the detection and application of IL-CDs.
Comparison of IL-CDs with other fluorescent probes.

The constant value of K
sv and K
q for the interaction between IL-CDs and AMY.
Detection of AMY in healthy human urine..

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescence and smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform based on ionic liquid carbon dots for rapid detection of amylase in urine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, Yan'an Street 2055, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, Yan'’an Street 2055, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Changes in amylase (AMY) concentration in urine serve a vital function in monitoring human health. However, the development of non-destructive, sensitive, and real-time assays for AMY concentration still faces serious challenges. In this study, a novel fluorescence/smartphone imaging colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform based on ionic liquid carbon dots (IL-CDs) was designed for the first time for assessing AMY concentration in urine. The obtained IL-CDs exhibited high sensitivity, good selectivity, and excellent stability. AMY can significantly decrease the fluorescence intensity through static quenching, enabling direct AMY concentration monitoring. Significantly, the proposed method was extended to the measurement of AMY in the urine samples with good spiked recoveries (96.98–103.81 %) as well as high accuracy. Furthermore, a smartphone-dependent detection method was developed after exploring the association between IL-CDs (G/(R + B)) fluorescence intensity at different concentrations of AMY, which simplified the AMY detection process. Through the combination of fluorescence and smartphone imaging colorimetry, it not only eliminated the damage to the human body during the detection process, but also realized the direct detection of AMY. The present method provided a fast and reliable new idea for monitoring human health.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Amylase (AMY) is an enzyme involved in digestion that breaks down starch into sugar and is primarily released by the salivary glands and pancreas [1]. It can break down the starch in the ingested food, ultimately converting it into energy substances for the body to absorb [2]. Premature intra-acinar activation of pancreatic zymogens initiates a proteolytic cascade, resulting in autodigestion of pancreatic parenchyma. After rupture of the acinar cells, AMY is released directly into the blood. Simultaneously, glomerular filtration rate is increased by acute inflammation, and urinary amylase excretion is accelerated [3]. Therefore, if AMY concentration is significantly increased in serum or urine, it may signal abnormal pancreatic function (pancreatitis, pancreatic cancer) [4]. If it fails to be diagnosed and treated in time, multiple organ dysfunction may be caused, thus posing a serious threat to the life and safety of patients [5–8]. Therefore, the detection of AMY concentration plays an important role in human health monitoring. However, conventional AMY assays necessitate invasive blood sampling, and the collection and handling process requires specialized techniques. Therefore, there is an urgent necessity to develop an accurate, rapid, and sensitive non-destructive method for the detection of AMY in urine as a complementary tool to clinical needs.

So far, various technical tools have been devised for the determination of AMY, such as spectrophotometry [9], electrochemical methods [10], electrophoresis methods [11], chromatographic methods [12], immunological methods [13], amperometric methods [14], isoelectric focusing methods [15], etc. Specifically, the spectrophotometric method is simpler to implement, but it requires a lengthy establishment process and offers relatively low accuracy [9]. The electrochemical method detects AMY by monitoring changes in charge density on the electrode, providing a high accuracy but poor selectivity [10,16]. The chromatography offers high resolution and the capability to separate multiple substances simultaneously at the expense of costly experimental equipment and poor qualitative ability [12]. On the other hand, the fluorescence approach has garnered significant attention owing to its excellent selectivity, accuracy, and sensitivity. To date, some studies have demonstrated fluorescence-based detection of AMY. Ananthaselvam et al. prepared poly-styrene nano plastic (PSNPs) and investigated its quenching effect with AMY in plants through fluorescence emission spectroscopy [17]. Julide et al. employed a fluorescence method based on starch‑iodine‑sodium fluorescein to measure the concentration of AMY in human serum [18]. However, these methods exhibit significant limitations in operation, lack of stability, and poor responsiveness. In fact, the preparation and selection of fluorescent materials are play a crucial role in the application of fluorescence methods.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel kind of carbon nanomaterial with special fluorescent properties, which have attracted close scrutiny from researchers in recent years. They feature a simple preparation process, low toxicity, wonderfully tunable photoluminescence, and excellent biocompatibility [19,20]. These advantages led to their important developments in numerous aspects, such as biosensing [21,22], photocatalysis [23], anti-counterfeiting [24], cell imaging [25–27], and drug delivery [28,29]. Moreover, excellent carbon precursors are particularly important in the preparation of CDs because they can significantly improve their physical, chemical, and optical properties [30,31]. Recently, ionic liquids (ILs) demonstrated great advantages in the preparation and post-modification of CDs resulting from excellent chemical and thermal stability, diverse selection of cationic and anionic moieties, and good solubility. Firstly, ILs exhibit low interfacial tension and interfacial energy, promote the rapid nucleation of CDs, and prevent the aggregation of nanoparticles. Additionally, the ILs precursor containing heteroatoms such as nitrogen, boron, and fluorine can augment the fluorescence quantum yield and enhance fluorescence signals of prepared IL-CDs [32–34]. Meanwhile, IL-CDs exhibit high stability and selectivity, which are suitable for the detection and analysis of target substances in complex matrices [35]. These advantages set the stage for IL-CDs to determine AMY.

However, due to the complexity of analytical procedures, fluorescence detection of CDs is limited to large laboratory equipment, lacks portability, and requires trained operators. In recent years, colorimetric biosensors have attracted significant research interest due to their simplicity of operation, portability, and visualization of detection [36]. Especially with the continued advancement of smartphones equipped with high-resolution cameras, it is widely recognized as a good point-of-care testing sensor analysis device [37]. To date, multiple smartphone detection platforms have been reported. Hao et al. used a smartphone imaging platform to detect nitrite in food [38]. Lu et al. used a smartphone colorimetric sensor to detect ampicillin in water samples for visual monitoring [39]. As a result, an integrated detection platform creation using a smartphone is highly beneficial for achieving effective, precise, and portable in situ AMY monitoring.

Based on the aforementioned considerations, a novel non-destructive and efficient fluorescence/smartphone imaging colorimetry dual-mode sensing platform was developed for the sensitive and selective detection of AMY in urine. The sustainability of this method was further investigated (Scheme 1 ). The sensor was prepared by 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate ([OMIM]BF4), 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (BTA), and m-phenylenediamine (MPD) as precursors. The prepared IL-CDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, excellent immunity to interference, and good fluorescence stability to AMY. Upon in-depth exploration of the mechanism of action, it was determined that static quenching led to a fluorescence decline. IL-CDs were further applied to real samples (human urine). Meanwhile, the fluorescence method was successfully integrated with a smartphone color recognition application for instant detection of AMY. The present method provides a non-invasive detection route, avoiding any harm to the human body during the detection process. It offers a simple and rapid solution that significantly enhances detection efficiency by directly targeting AMY for precise detection, thus substantially improving the convenience and safety of the detection process. The detection of AMY in urine by IL-CDs-based sensor platform is expected to become a new tool for the early diagnosis of pancreatitis.

[OMIM]PF4, BTA, and MPD were purchased from Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). AMY (50 U/mg), pepsin (Pep), lipase (Lip), and trypsin (Try) were from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chemical reagents such as sodium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium sulfate, zinc acetate, ferric nitrate, and barium sulfate were acquired from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100F) was applied to study the size and morphology of IL-CDs. Elemental content was measured using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Malvern Panalytical，X‘Pert3 MRD). A Raman spectrometer (HORIBA, LabRAM HR Evolution) was applied to collect Raman spectra. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectral analysis was characterized on an infrared spectrophotometer (thermostat, Nicolet IS50). A fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, LS-55) applied reveals fluorescence spectra. An X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, SmartLab-9 kW) was used to confirm the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra.

[OMIM]PF4, BTA, and MPD were used as precursors in a one-step hydrothermal process to create IL-CDs with green fluorescence emission. To guarantee uniform dissolution, 0.1 g of MPD, 0.5 g of [OMIM]PF4, and 0.25 g BTA were first combined with 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Afterwords, the mixed solution was placed into a 50 mL autoclave lined with Teflon and heated (180 °C, 12 h) in an oven. The acquired dark green IL-CDs solution was naturally cooled and then centrifuged through a high-speed centrifuge at 10,000 rmp for 10 min and filtered (0.22 μm). Finally, the solution was purified by dialysis bags (500 Da) for 24 h and then freeze-dried.

At room temperature, the produced IL-CDs solution was employed as a fluorescent probe to detect AMY. 10 μL of IL-CDs was thoroughly mixed with 100 μL of an array of AMY standard solutions of different concentrations (0–0.01 g/mL), and then the final volume of 3 mL was adjusted with water. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm was recorded and the sensitivity of IL-CDs to AMY was assessed. In addition, the selectivity for various interfering ions (Na+、K+、Mg2+、Zn2+、Fe2+、Fe3+、Ba2+、Cu2+、Mn2+、Al3+、Ag+) as well as interfering enzymes (Lip、Pep、Try) was determined by the same method.

The pretreatment method for real samples is described in section S2.1 of the Supplementary material. 10 μL of IL-CDs solution was mixed with 100 μL of urine sample solution and adjusted with water to a total volume of 3 mL. The mixture was poured into a cuvette and placed in a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrum at 440 nm excitation wavelength was recorded. Three replicate measurements were used in recovery experiments at three peak levels (low, medium, and high) to determine recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD).

After adding different concentrations of AMY to the IL-CDs solution, the cuvette containing the reaction system was stored in the dark. Images were captured using a smartphone (Apple iPhone 16 Pro Max, 48 megapixels) under UV light, at a fixed angle and orientation. To minimize the background interference, the experiments were performed in the dark by means of a UV lamp with a white bulb to simulate the UV and visible light, respectively. Red/R, green/G, and blue/B values were extracted using a color recognition application, and then a linear relationship between the G/(R + B) ratio and AMY concentration was calculated and established.

The greenness level of the developed AMY assays was evaluated with the two programs Analytical Greenness Calculator (AGREE) and Analytical GREEnness metric (AGREEprep), respectively. The assessment covered criteria as shown in S2.2 in the supplementary information. Finally, an average score was calculated based on these criteria to reflect the green level of the method.

The TEM image evidenced that IL-CDs exhibit approximately spherical particles with a uniform distribution and regular shape (Fig. 1 ). Inset of Fig. 1A was the HR-TEM image, which depicted clear lattice fringes with a d-spacing of about 0.15 nm. The particle size of IL-CDs ranged from 0.68 to 2.71 nm, with an average particle size of 1.65 ± 0.56 nm (Fig. 1B) [40]. The broad XRD peak of IL-CDs at 2θ = 20.4°, which corresponded to the lattice structure in the graphite plane of (002) (Fig. 1C). The stretching vibration of CH could be observed at 2925 cm−1 [41], as depicted in Fig. 1D. The stretching vibration of CO was represented by the peak at 1724 cm−1 [42]. The stretching vibration peaks of CF and CO were identified as the source of the peaks at 1243 cm−1 and 1034 cm−1, respectively [43]. Additionally, the structure of IL-CDs was further characterized by Raman spectroscopy. IL-CDs was observed in two characteristic signal bands at 1363 cm−1 (disordered D band) and 1575 cm−1 (graphite G band) under excitation with a wavelength of 450 nm (Fig. S1). Corresponding to the sp2 disordered heterostructure and sp3 lattice heterostructure of carbon, respectively. [44].

The surface composition of IL-CDs were probed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectrum illustrated that five peaks appear at 195 eV, 285 eV, 402 eV, 533 eV, and 686 eV, respectively, matching C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, F 1 s, and B 1 s elements (Fig. 2A). The contents of each element are C (66.44 %), N (5.78 %), O (15.23 %), F (8.97 %), and B (3.59 %), respectively. The C 1 s spectrum revealed three different peaks (Fig. 2B) at 284.8 eV (CC), 286.3 eV (CN), and 289.0 eV (CO) [45]. The peaks at 399.4 eV and 401.7 eV in the N 1S spectrum were related to the CN and NH structures, respectively (Fig. 2C) [46]. The O 1 s spectrum exhibited two peaks located at 532.1 eV and 533.4 eV, which were ascribed to the CO structure and the CO structure, respectively (Fig. 2D) [47]. In Fig. 2E, the F 1 s spectrum was separated into two peaks at 685.4 eV and 686.1 eV, which were attributed to the peaks of semi-ionic and covalently bonded CF [48]. Meanwhile, the two peaks located at 193.7 eV and 194.2 eV are assigned to BC and BO bonds (Fig. 2F) [49]. According to the XPS data, B, F, and N atoms were effectively incorporated into IL-CDs, and their surface were abundant with functional groups such as CO, CF and CO. The XPS results was consistent with the above-mentioned infrared spectrum analysis results.

UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy were utilized to characterize the optical properties. IL-CDs displayed prominent absorption peaks at 245 nm and 280 nm, respectively (Fig. 3A). The former corresponded to the π-π* transition of the CC bond, and the latter represented the n-π* absorption in the CO bond [50]. The IL-CDs solution was almost transparent and colorless under natural light but displayed green fluorescence when exposed to UV light. The optimal excitation wavelength was 440 nm, and the emission wavelength was 511 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum of IL-CDs in the 400–460 nm excitation wavelength range was displayed in Fig. 3B. The IL-CDs exhibited significant excitation-independent characteristics. The fluorescence intensity initially increased and then decreased with increasing excitation wavelength. Moreover, transient fluorescence spectrometry revealed that the IL-CDs possessed an absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 21.58 %. Fig. S2 displayed an average fluorescence lifetime of 0.40 ns.

To evaluate long-term stability, the IL-CDs solution was stored under light-avoidance conditions with daily monitoring of fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. S3, the IL-CDs maintained stable fluorescence intensity over 28 days, demonstrating exceptional long-term stability. The stability of IL-CDs in different environments was researched by examining the effects of different pH, salt concentration, UV irradiation time and temperature on the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 4A demonstrated the changes in fluorescence intensity of the IL-CDs solution under different pH. The fluorescence intensity increased progressively, approaching its maximum at pH 7.0, and then gradually reduced. Owing to deprotonation under alkaline environment, the fluorescence nearly vanished at pH 12–14 [51]. Afterwards, a certain amount of NaCl solution (1–10−7 mol/L) of different concentrations was added to investigate the impact of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 4B illustrated that the fluorescence intensity of IL-CDs remained significant even at a NaCl concentration of 1 mol/L. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity showed negligible change after sustained continuous UV irradiation for 100 min (Fig. 4C). Besides, the variation in fluorescence intensity remained negligible over the temperature range of 20–80 °C. According to the experimental results, the prepared IL-CDs solution demonstrated strong resistance to photobleaching, high temperature, salt tolerance, and pH stability. These features afforded IL-CDs the potential to be employed for AMY analysis.

The selectivity of IL-CDs in the company of other possible simultaneous interferents was evaluated in order to illustrate the possibility of distinct recognition of IL-CDs. In Fig. 5A, when different interferents (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ag+, Try, Pep, Lip) at identical concentrations were added to the IL-CDs solution after incubation for 20 min, negligible fluorescence quenching was observed. However, the fluorescence was more obviously quenched in the presence of AMY. It signaled that IL-CDs displayed excellent selectivity for AMY enabling specific detection of AMY in urine samples without interference from other coexisting substances.

Different concentrations of AMY were included in the IL-CDs solution in order to investigate the sensitivity of IL-CDs to AMY, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded under optimal experimental conditions. As the concentration of AMY grew from 0 to 0.01 g/mL, the fluorescence intensity of IL-CDs gradually diminished (Fig. 5B). The fluorescence intensity and the AMY concentration displayed a significant linear correlation. The regression equation was fitted as Y = −2.30 × 107 X + 1,164,345.91 (R2 = 0.9995), where X stood for AMY concentration and Y stood for IL-CDs fluorescence intensity. The LOD was calculated to be 0.015 μg/mL based on LOD = 3σ/k, where k represented the slope of the linear regression equation and σ signified the standard deviations (n = 11) of the AMY-free solution. The results suggested that the present method exhibited both excellent selectivity and high sensitivity, enabling specific AMY quantification.

To evaluate the performance of this method, it was in comparison with reported AMY detection methods [52–54]. As shown in Table 1 , this method exhibited faster detection speed, shorter response time, lower LOD, and wider detection range compared with other methods.

In order to research the quenching mechanism, the UV–visible absorption spectrum, infrared spectrum, fluorescence lifetime, and Stern-Volmer equation were investigated. In Fig. 6A, a characteristic absorption peak at 255 nm emerged in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AMY. This peak exhibited no significant overlap with the excitation peak (440 nm) and emission peak (511 nm) of IL-CDs, suggesting that neither internal filtration effect (IFE) nor Foster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) was responsible for the fluorescence quenching effect of IL-CDs. Thus, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of AMY detection by IL-CDs may involve either dynamic quenching or static quenching. However, the fluorescence lifetime curve of IL-CDs before and after the addition of AMY is demonstrated in Fig. 6B. The measured fluorescence lifetime of IL-CDs was 0.40 ns, while adding AMY exhibited no discernible effect on the fluorescence lifetime (0.37 ns). This result clearly eliminated the possibility of dynamic quenching mechanism. Fig. 6C exhibited a distinct shift in the 280 nm absorption peak of IL-CDs after AMY adding, which suggested significant alterations in the n-π* electronic transition characteristics of CO bonds. Fig. 6D demonstrated that the CO stretching vibration of IL-CDs underwent a significant red shift from 1724 cm−1 to 1638 cm−1 upon AMY binding. The UV–vis and FTIR results indicated that ground-state complexes were formed between AMY and the CO groups of IL-CDs through hydrogen bonding, leading to a decrease in the stretching vibration intensity of the CO bonds. Consequently, the quenching of IL-CDs by AMY likely occurs via static quenching. Furthermore, the quenching mechanism was further validated by employing the Stern-Volmer equation. The following Stern-Volmer (SV) equation can be applied to get the fluorescence quenching constant:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
where τ0 denoted the lifetime of IL-CDs without adding a quencher. [Q] signified the concentration of AMY. K
q and K
sv represented the bimolecular reaction rate constant and the quenching constant of the IL-CDs-AMY, respectively. F and F
0 signified the fluorescence intensity of IL-CDs with and without AMY, respectively. The temperature dependence of the fluorescence quenching effect was investigated. The corresponding SV curves were drawn (Fig. 6E), and three SV curves exhibited a great linear relationship. The slope represented the Ksv, and the Kq value was calculated from τ0 and Ksv values using the SV equation. According to the data in Table 2 , the Ksv value indicated a downward trend with increasing temperature (Ksv (30 °C) > Ksv (40 °C) > (50 °C)). The Kq value was much higher than the reported maximum collision quenching constant (2.0 × 1010 g/mL/s), which also proved that the quenching of IL-CDs by AMY conformed to the static quenching mechanism [55]. Overall, the experimental results strongly confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of IL-CDs by AMY occurred via static quenching (Fig. 6F).

Recovery experiments were conducted using the standard addition method with varying concentrations of AMY in real urine samples to evaluate the applicability of the proposed fluorescence method for the complex sample analysis. Three spiked concentration levels of AMY were set as 0.0001 g/mL, 0.001 g/mL, and 0.01 g/mL. The recoveries of AMY spiked in the urine of healthy individuals were listed in Table 3 , with a range of 96.98–103.81 % and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.72–3.26 % (n = 3). Detection of AMY in pancreatitis patient urine was presented in Table S1. The results demonstrated that IL-CDs demonstrated high accuracy and reproducibility, suggesting their potential for practical application in the detection of AMY.

Experiments were performed with varying AMY concentrations in the range of 0 to 0.1 g/mL in order to further enhance the detection capabilities of IL-CDs under elevated concentration circumstances. Notably, the color of the IL-CDs solution gradually faded from green under 245 nm UV light as depicted in Fig. S4. Based on this consideration, a convenient identification strategy for AMY was developed using smartphone imaging. Fig. 7 illustrated the specific steps of the smartphone detection platform. Firstly, a series of fluorescent images was captured by the smartphone camera. Subsequently, the photos colors were transformed into the corresponding RGB data by a smartphone APP (Real-time color picker). As a result, the G/(R + B) ratio showed excellent linear correlation with AMY concentration, described by G/(R + B) = −4.0923× + 0.9885 (R2 = 0.9957) with a detection limit (LOD) of 1.3 μg/mL. Therefore, this smartphone-based approach enables visual quantification of AMY concentrations, overcoming the limitations of conventional laboratory instrumentation. The developed method significantly simplifies AMY detection, providing a rapid and portable solution for on-site monitoring and real-time analysis.

The greenness of the method was evaluated using AGREE (Analytical Greenness Calculator) [56] and AGREEprep (Analytical Greenness Metric) [57]. Greenness for each indicator was measured by a standardised score (0–1), which was positively correlated with sustainability performance. Higher scores represented better greenness performance. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the greenness score of this method was 0.8 (AGREE) and 0.86 (AGREEprep) derived from the calculation of each parameter, respectively. The pictogram assessment demonstrated this method featured simple operation with a small solvent amount and low energy consumption. It is an efficient and environmentally sustainable method for the determination of AMY. The whole preparation and analysis process has minimal impact on the environment.

The cytotoxicity of IL-CDs was investigated by MTT test. Even at high concentration of 1.60 mg/mL, the 24-h survival of both cells (Hela and MCF-7) was higher than 80 % (Hela and MCF-7) (Fig. S5). Therefore, the excellent biosafety of IL-CDs was sufficiently confirmed, which demonstrated significant potential for biomedical applications.

In summary, a novel, efficient, and non-destructive sensing platform for the quantification of AMY in urine was established. The IL-CDs exhibited tunable photoluminescence, good fluorescence stability, high selectivity and specificity. A new chemical bond was formed between the added AMY and IL-CDs, which contributed to the decline in fluorescence intensity of IL-CDs through static quenching. In addition, IL-CDs were further achieved for sensitive detection of AMY in urine with high accuracy and recovery (96.98–103.81 %). Furthermore, the smartphone-based sensing platform enables visual colorimetric analysis of AMY, realizing a streamlined and efficient process. The sensor achieved non-destructive, real-time direct detection of AMY concentration, providing a rapid and feasible new strategy for quantifying AMY concentration in real urine samples.

Xiujuan Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiarui Wang: Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Haojia Tian: Validation, Methodology. Chenzhao Wang: Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xiaowen Qiu: Validation, Methodology. Zhicong Li: Validation, Methodology. Zhibing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Research Fund for Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Department (No.20240303057NC) and Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission (No.2024C016-4).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.143654.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Starch digestion in the upper gastrointestinal tract of humans

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.A. Brownlee, S. Gill, M.D. Wilcox, J.P. Pearson, P.I. Chater, Starch digestion in the upper gastrointestinal tract of humans, Starch - Stärke 70(9-10) (2018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Α-Amylase expressed in human small intestinal epithelial cells is essential for cell proliferation and differentiation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Date, T. Yamazaki, Y. Toyoda, K. Hoshi, H. Ogawa, α-Amylase expressed in human small intestinal epithelial cells is essential for cell proliferation and differentiation, Journal of Cellular Biochemistry 121(2) (2019) 1238-1249.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Progress of pancreatitis disease biomarker alpha amylase enzyme by new nano optical sensor
